content:
# John Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 5 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 5:1 — "a feast of the Jews" vs. "one of the Jewish festivals"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there was a feast of the Ioudaioi, and the Yeshua went up to Hierosolyma" | Literal; transliterated terms |
| LOGOS | "there was a feast of the Jews, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus went up to Jerusalem for one of the Jewish festivals." | Dynamic; "one of the Jewish festivals" specifies indefiniteness; reorders |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "one of the Jewish festivals" is a reasonable interpretation of the anarthrous ἑορτή but adds specificity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:2 — "in Aramaic called Bethesda" vs. "called Bethesda" — language note

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] called in Hebrew Bethesda, having five porticoes" | "Hebrew"; "porticoes" |
| LOGOS | "in Aramaic called Bethesda, which has five roofed colonnades" | "Aramaic" (correcting the Greek τῇ Ἑβραϊστί); "roofed colonnades" |
| PNEUMA | "a pool called Bethesda, with five covered walkways surrounding it" | Omits language note; "covered walkways"; adds "surrounding it" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek says "in Hebrew" but the name is Aramaic — LOGOS correctly notes this. PNEUMA omits the language reference entirely and adds "surrounding it."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:3 — "invalids" vs. "sick" vs. "sick people" and "paralyzed" vs. "withered"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a multitude of the sick — blind, lame, withered" | "Withered" for ξηρῶν |
| LOGOS | "a multitude of invalids — blind, lame, and paralyzed" | "Invalids"; "paralyzed" |
| PNEUMA | "Crowds of sick people — blind, lame, paralyzed — lay on the walkways" | "Crowds"; adds "lay on the walkways" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. EMET's "withered" (ξηρός = dried up/withered) is more literal but less clear. PNEUMA adds "lay on the walkways" not in this verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:6 — "Do you want to be healed?" vs. "Do you wish to become well?" vs. "Would you like to get well?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do you wish to become well?" | Literal; "wish" for θέλεις |
| LOGOS | "Do you want to be healed?" | Formal; "healed" |
| PNEUMA | "Would you like to get well?" | Dynamic; softened question form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Healed" is more direct and medically appropriate. PNEUMA's "Would you like" softens the directness of Jesus' question.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 5:7 — "Sir" vs. "Lord" for κύριε; "I have no one to put me into the pool"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord, I do not have a man that when the water is stirred he may cast me into the pool." | "Lord"; literal structure |
| LOGOS | "Sir, I have no one to put me into the pool when the water is stirred up" | "Sir" |
| PNEUMA | "'I can't, sir,' the sick man said, 'because I have no one to put me into the pool when the water bubbles up.'" | Adds "I can't"; "bubbles up" for stirred |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "I can't" which is not in the Greek — the man does not directly answer Jesus' question in the original text. "Bubbles up" for ταραχή (stirring/disturbance) is interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:8 — "Get up, take up your bed, and walk" vs. "Rise, take up the mat" vs. "Stand up, pick up your mat, and walk!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Rise, take up the mat of you and walk." | Literal; "mat" for κράβαττον |
| LOGOS | "Get up, take up your bed, and walk." | Formal; "bed" |
| PNEUMA | "Stand up, pick up your mat, and walk!" | Dynamic; exclamation mark |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. κράβαττος is a pallet/mat/cot — "bed" is the standard translation. PNEUMA's exclamation mark adds emphasis not marked in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 5:9 — "at once" vs. "immediately" vs. "Instantly"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And immediately the man became well" | "Immediately" |
| LOGOS | "And at once the man was healed" | "At once" |
| PNEUMA | "Instantly, the man was healed! He rolled up his mat and began walking." | "Instantly!"; adds "He rolled up" and "began walking" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "rolled up" (not in Greek) and converts the narrative to a more vivid scene.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:14 — "See, you are well!" vs. "Now you are well." vs. "Behold, you have become well."

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, you have become well. Sin no longer, lest something worse happen to you." | "Behold"; "Sin no longer" |
| LOGOS | "See, you are well! Sin no more, that nothing worse may happen to you." | "See"; "Sin no more" |
| PNEUMA | "Now you are well. Stop sinning, or something even worse may happen to you." | "Now"; "Stop sinning" — more colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "Stop sinning" is colloquial but captures the imperative. "Now" loses the demonstrative force of ἴδε ("see/behold").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 5:16 — "persecuting" vs. "harassing" for Greek ἐδίωκον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ioudaioi were persecuting the Yeshua" | "Persecuting" |
| LOGOS | "the Jews were persecuting Jesus" | "Persecuting" |
| PNEUMA | "the Jewish leaders began harassing Jesus for breaking the Sabbath rules" | "Harassing"; adds "leaders" and "rules" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. διώκω means "pursue/persecute" — "harassing" is weaker. PNEUMA adds "leaders" and "rules" (not in the text).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:17 — "My Father is working until now" vs. "My Father is always working"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Father of me works until now, and I work." | Literal; "until now" for ἕως ἄρτι |
| LOGOS | "My Father is working until now, and I am working." | Formal; progressive tense |
| PNEUMA | "My Father is always working, and so am I." | Dynamic; "always" replaces "until now" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Until now" (ἕως ἄρτι) has a specific temporal sense — from creation until this moment. PNEUMA's "always" loses the temporal specificity of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:19 — "the Son can do nothing of his own accord" vs. "the Son can do nothing by himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Son is not able to do from himself anything, unless he sees the Father doing" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the Son can do nothing by himself. He does only what he sees the Father doing." | Dynamic; splits into two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's sentence splitting is acceptable but loses the single-sentence unity of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 5:22 — "has given all judgment to the Son" vs. "has given the Son absolute authority to judge"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has given all the judgment to the Son" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "has given all judgment to the Son" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "has given the Son absolute authority to judge" | Dynamic; "absolute authority" interprets πᾶσαν τὴν κρίσιν |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "absolute authority" is interpretive — the Greek says "all judgment" (πᾶσαν τὴν κρίσιν), not "absolute authority."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:24 — "has passed from death to life" vs. "have already passed from death into life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has passed from the death into the life" | Literal; articles |
| LOGOS | "has passed from death to life" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "They will never be condemned for their sins, but they have already passed from death into life." | Dynamic; adds "for their sins"; "already" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "for their sins" (not in the Greek) and "already" (adding emphasis).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:29 — "resurrection of judgment" vs. "experience judgment" for ἀνάστασιν κρίσεως

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to [a] resurrection of judgment" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "to the resurrection of judgment" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "will rise to experience judgment" | Dynamic; "experience" instead of "resurrection of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The phrase "resurrection of judgment" preserves the parallel structure with "resurrection of life." PNEUMA's "rise to experience" loses this parallelism.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:35 — "a burning and shining lamp" vs. "like a burning and shining lamp"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "That [one] was the lamp burning and shining, and you wished for an hour to rejoice in the light of him." | Literal; "the lamp" with article |
| LOGOS | "He was a burning and shining lamp, and you were willing to rejoice for a while in his light." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "John was like a burning and shining lamp, and you were excited for a while about his message." | Dynamic; adds "like"; "excited"; "message" instead of "light" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "like" (the Greek says John WAS the lamp, not was LIKE a lamp). "Excited" is weaker than "willing to rejoice" (ἠθελήσατε ἀγαλλιαθῆναι). "Message" substitutes for "light" (φῶς).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:37 — "His voice you have never heard, his form you have never seen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Neither the voice of him have you ever heard, nor the form of him have you seen." | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "His voice you have never heard, his form you have never seen" | Formal; preserves emphatic front-position |
| PNEUMA | "You have never heard his voice or seen him face to face" | Dynamic; "face to face" replaces "form" (εἶδος) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "face to face" is interpretive — εἶδος means "form/appearance," not "face."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:39 — "You search the Scriptures" vs. "You search the Scriptures because you think they give you eternal life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You search the scriptures, because you think in them to have eternal life, and those are the [ones] testifying concerning me." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "You search the Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life; and it is they that bear witness about me" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "You search the Scriptures because you think they give you eternal life. But the Scriptures point to me!" | Dynamic; splits; adds exclamation; "point to me" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "point to me!" with exclamation mark adds dramatic emphasis. The Greek is a declarative statement, not an exclamation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:44 — "the glory that comes from the only God" vs. "the praise that comes from the one who alone is God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the glory from the only God you do not seek" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "do not seek the glory that comes from the only God" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "you don't care about the praise that comes from the one who alone is God" | Dynamic; "don't care about" for "do not seek"; "praise" for "glory" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Glory" (δόξα) is the correct rendering for this Johannine term. PNEUMA's "praise" and "don't care about" are colloquial reductions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 10 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 7 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 7 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Word choice divergence (all three) | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Verse 4 omission | 1 | Consensus (all follow Sinaiticus) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 5 is dominated by Jesus' discourse on his relationship to the Father (vv.19-47), which is one of the most theologically precise passages in the NT. The translators show strong agreement on the core theological vocabulary but diverge in structural and stylistic choices. PNEUMA's most notable departures include rendering ἕως ἄρτι as "always" (losing temporal specificity in v.17), adding "absolute authority" for "all judgment" (v.22), and replacing "form" with "face to face" (v.37). EMET preserves Greek word order with particular effectiveness in v.37, where the emphatic front-positioning of "neither the voice...nor the form" reinforces the rhetorical power.

All three translators correctly handle the omission of verse 4 (one of the clearest scribal interpolations in the NT) and agree on the indicative reading of "You search" in verse 39.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
